From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Indicates an intentional reproduction in quotation

For other uses, see [SIC (disambiguation)](/wiki/SIC_(disambiguation) "SIC (disambiguation)").

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg/40px-Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg)

Look up ***[sic](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sic#Etymology_1 "wiktionary:sic")*** in Wiktionary, the free dictionary.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Example_use_of_sic.svg/500px-Example_use_of_sic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Example_use_of_sic.svg)

Example of "sic" being used after a word in a quotation or passage, to indicate that the quoted matter has been transcribed or translated exactly as found in the original. Example is from a United States Supreme Court case, *Briggs v. Connecticut*, [447](/wiki/List_of_United_States_Supreme_Court_cases,_volume_447 "List of United States Supreme Court cases, volume 447") [U.S.](/wiki/United_States_Reports "United States Reports") [912](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/447/912/) (1980).

The [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") [adverb](/wiki/Adverb "Adverb") ***sic*** ([/sɪk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); 'thus', 'so', and 'in this manner') is inserted after a quotation to indicate that the quoted matter has been transcribed or translated as found in the original source, including erroneous, archaic, or unusual spelling, [punctuation](/wiki/Punctuation "Punctuation"), and [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar").[[1]](#cite_note-Garner_Page_999-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) *Sic* also applies to any surprising assertion, faulty reasoning, or other matter that might otherwise be interpreted as an [error of transcription](/wiki/Transcription_error "Transcription error").

The typical editorial usage of *sic* is to inform the reader that any errors in a quotation did not arise from editorial errors in the transcription, but are intentionally reproduced as they appear in the original source being quoted; thus, *sic* is placed inside [brackets](/wiki/Bracket#Square_brackets "Bracket") to indicate it is not part of the quotation.[[1]](#cite_note-Garner_Page_999-1) *Sic* can also be used derisively to direct the reader's attention to the writer's spelling mistakes and erroneous logic, or to show disapproval of the content or form of the material.[[1]](#cite_note-Garner_Page_999-1)[[4]](#cite_note-garner2001-4)

## Etymology and historical usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and historical usage")]

In the English language, the Latin adverb *sic* is used as an adverb, and derivatively as a noun and as a verb.[[5]](#cite_note-oed1989-5) The [adverb](/wiki/Adverb "Adverb") *sic*, meaning 'intentionally so written', first appeared in English c. 1856.[[6]](#cite_note-webster2003-6) It is derived from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") adverb *sīc*, which means 'so', 'thus', 'in this manner'.[[7]](#cite_note-7) According to the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, the verbal form of *sic*, meaning 'to mark with a *sic*', emerged in 1889, [E. Belfort Bax](/wiki/E._Belfort_Bax "E. Belfort Bax")'s work in *The Ethics of Socialism* being an early example.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Folk etymologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Folk etymologies")]

On occasion, *sic* has been misidentified as an [acronym](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym")[[9]](#cite_note-9) (and therefore sometimes misspelled with periods): *s.i.c.* is said to stand for "spelled/said in copy/context", "spelling is correct", "spelled incorrectly", and other such [folk etymology](/wiki/Folk_etymology "Folk etymology") phrases. These are all incorrect and are simply [backronyms](/wiki/Backronym "Backronym") from *sic*.

## Modern usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Modern usage")]

Use of *sic* greatly increased in the mid-20th century.[[10]](#cite_note-garner2000-10) For example, in [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") state-court opinions before 1944, *sic* appeared 1,239 times in the [Westlaw](/wiki/Westlaw "Westlaw") database; in those from 1945 to 1990, it appeared 69,168 times, over 55 times as many.[[4]](#cite_note-garner2001-4) Its use as a form of ridicule has been cited as a major factor in this increase.[[4]](#cite_note-garner2001-4) The immoderate use of *sic* has created some controversy, leading some editors, including bibliographical scholar Simon Nowell-Smith and literary critic [Leon Edel](/wiki/Leon_Edel "Leon Edel"), to speak out against it.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Conventional use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Conventional use")]

The bracketed form [*sic*] is most often inserted into quoted or reprinted material to indicate meticulous accuracy in reproducing the preceding text, despite appearances to the reader of an incorrect or unusual [orthography](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") (spelling, [punctuation](/wiki/Punctuation "Punctuation"), etc.), grammar, syntax, fact, or logic.[[4]](#cite_note-garner2001-4)[[12]](#cite_note-usd2009-12) Several usage guides recommend that a bracketed *sic* be used primarily as an aid to the reader, not as an indicator of disagreement with the source.[[4]](#cite_note-garner2001-4)[[13]](#cite_note-coyle2009-13)

### Use to denote archaisms and dialect

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Use to denote archaisms and dialect")]

*Sic* may show that an uncommon or [archaic](/wiki/Archaism "Archaism") expression is reported faithfully,[[14]](#cite_note-14) such as when quoting the [U.S. Constitution](/wiki/U.S._Constitution "U.S. Constitution"): "The House of Representatives shall chuse [*sic*] their Speaker ..." However, several writing guidebooks discourage its use with regard to dialect, such as in cases of [American and British English spelling differences](/wiki/American_and_British_English_spelling_differences "American and British English spelling differences").[[10]](#cite_note-garner2000-10)[[13]](#cite_note-coyle2009-13)[[15]](#cite_note-15) The appearance of a bracketed *sic* after the word *[analyse](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/analyse "wikt:analyse")* in a book review led [Bryan A. Garner](/wiki/Bryan_A._Garner "Bryan A. Garner") to comment, "all the quoter (or overzealous editor) demonstrated was ignorance of British usage".[[4]](#cite_note-garner2001-4)

### Ironic use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ironic use")]

See also: [Scare quotes](/wiki/Scare_quotes "Scare quotes")

Occasionally a writer places [*sic*] after their own words, to indicate that the language has been chosen deliberately for special effect, especially where the writer's ironic meaning may otherwise be unclear.[[16]](#cite_note-fowler1929-16) [Bryan A. Garner](/wiki/Bryan_A._Garner "Bryan A. Garner") dubbed this use of *sic* "ironic", providing the following example from [Fred Rodell](/wiki/Fred_Rodell "Fred Rodell")'s 1955 book *Nine Men*:[[4]](#cite_note-garner2001-4)

> [I]n 1951, it was the blessing bestowed on Judge [Harold Medina](/wiki/Harold_Medina "Harold Medina")'s *prosecution*[[a]](#cite_note-17) [*sic*] of the eleven so-called 'top native Communists,' which blessing meant giving the Smith Act the judicial nod of constitutionality.

## Formatting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Formatting")]

Where *sic* follows the quotation, it takes [brackets](/wiki/Bracket "Bracket"): [*sic*].[[17]](#cite_note-Jessen-18) The word *sic* is often treated as a [loanword](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") that does not require italics, and the style manuals of New Zealand, Australian and British media outlets generally do not require italicisation.[[13]](#cite_note-coyle2009-13) However, italicization is common in the United States, where authorities including *[APA Style](/wiki/APA_Style "APA Style")* insist upon it.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

Because *sic* is not an abbreviation, placing a [full stop](/wiki/Full_stop "Full stop")/period inside the brackets after the word *sic* is erroneous,[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-cgsau-21) although the California Style Manual suggests styling it as a parenthetical sentence only when used after a complete sentence, like so: (*Sic.*)[[17]](#cite_note-Jessen-18)

## Alternatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Alternatives")]

### Replacement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Replacement")]

Some guides, including *[The Chicago Manual of Style](/wiki/The_Chicago_Manual_of_Style "The Chicago Manual of Style")*, recommend "quiet [copy-editing](/wiki/Copy-editing "Copy-editing")" (unless where inappropriate or uncertain) instead of inserting a bracketed *sic*, such as by substituting in brackets the correct word in place of the incorrect word or by simply replacing an incorrect spelling with the correct one.[[4]](#cite_note-garner2001-4)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

### *Recte*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Recte")]

Alternatively, to show both the original and the suggested correction (as they often are in [palaeography](/wiki/Palaeography "Palaeography")), one may use the Latin adverb *recte* (meaning *rightly*[[22]](#cite_note-23)), in the form of writing the actual form, followed by *recte* and the correct form together in brackets. For example:

> An Iraqi battalion has consumed [*recte* assumed] control of the former American military base, and our forces are now about 40 minutes outside the city.

According to the *Journal of Seventeenth-Century Music Style Sheet*, there should be no punctuation, for example no colon, before the correct form when using *recte*.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

### Read

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sic&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Read")]

A third alternative is to follow an error with *sic*, a comma or colon, "read", and the correct reading, all within square brackets, as in the following example:

> Item 26 - 'Plan of space alongside Evinghews [sic: read Evening News] Printing Works and overlooked by St. Giles House University Hall', [Edinburgh][[24]](#cite_note-25)
